coredata.dc:title: High availability and outstanding catalytic activity in sodium borohydride hydrolytic dehydrogenation of CQD/GO@Co catalyst by green synthesis: Experimental and computational perspective

coredata.dc:description: This study is a pioneering study that experimentally and theoretically measures the effect of heterogeneous catalyst structure on hydrogen production. In this research, the catalytic activity of the (CQD/GO@Co) catalyst, which was formed by doping Co onto the synthesized graphene oxide (GO) on carbon quantum dots (CQD) obtained from caffeine by green synthesis, was investigated. The catalyst CQD/GO@Co was synthesized with high dispersibility and activity, and it was successfully employed in the hydrolysis of NaBH4 for the first time. The effective catalytic compatibility of caffeine and cobalt element increased approximately 3 times on the GO layer. In order to determine the most effective catalyst conditions in the study, NaOH concentration, catalyst amount, NaBH4 amount and temperature were tested under different reactant conditions. The catalyst was characterized using X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyses. Field emission scanning electron micrographs (FESEM) were taken by Field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Sigma). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra were obtained from Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope. According to spherical aberration transmission electron microscopy studies, the uniform dispersion of Co nanoparticles (average diameter: ±3,1 nm) on CQDs, as well as the very small size and good dispersion of Cobalt (Co), are conducive to improved catalytic performance. This situation was determined by molecular modeling with the Non-scc GFN1xTB model and results supporting the experimental data were obtained. The maximum hydrogen production rate with sodium borohydride hydrolysis using cobalt-based catalyst increased approximately 32 times (from metallic crystal form of Co catalyst 2860 mL/min.(g.cat) to the present form 49,044 mL/min.(g.cat). The activation energy, activation enthalpy, and activation entropy of the Co@CQDs catalyst were founded to be 20.65 kJ mol−1, 2.64 kJ mol−1, and -108,97 J mol−1, respectively.

head.ce:title: High availability and outstanding catalytic activity in sodium borohydride hydrolytic dehydrogenation of CQD/GO@Co catalyst by green synthesis: Experimental and computational perspective

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study is a pioneering study that experimentally and theoretically measures the effect of heterogeneous catalyst structure on hydrogen production. In this research, the catalytic activity of the (CQD/GO@Co) catalyst, which was formed by doping Co onto the synthesized graphene oxide (GO) on carbon quantum dots (CQD) obtained from caffeine by green synthesis, was investigated. The catalyst CQD/GO@Co was synthesized with high dispersibility and activity, and it was successfully employed in the hydrolysis of NaBH4 for the first time. The effective catalytic compatibility of caffeine and cobalt element increased approximately 3 times on the GO layer. In order to determine the most effective catalyst conditions in the study, NaOH concentration, catalyst amount, NaBH4 amount and temperature were tested under different reactant conditions. The catalyst was characterized using X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyses. Field emission scanning electron micrographs (FESEM) were taken by Field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Sigma). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra were obtained from Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope. According to spherical aberration transmission electron microscopy studies, the uniform dispersion of Co nanoparticles (average diameter: ±3,1 nm) on CQDs, as well as the very small size and good dispersion of Cobalt (Co), are conducive to improved catalytic performance. This situation was determined by molecular modeling with the Non-scc GFN1xTB model and results supporting the experimental data were obtained. The maximum hydrogen production rate with sodium borohydride hydrolysis using cobalt-based catalyst increased approximately 32 times (from metallic crystal form of Co catalyst 2860 mL/min.(g.cat) to the present form 49,044 mL/min.(g.cat). The activation energy, activation enthalpy, and activation entropy of the Co@CQDs catalyst were founded to be 20.65 kJ mol−1, 2.64 kJ mol−1, and -108,97 J mol−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para:
As it is known, hydrogen, one of the clean energy sources; There has been a remarkable increase in the use of energy recently, as it has clean, environmentally friendly products and the possibility of recycling [1–5]. Hydrogen energy is usually converted to electrical energy by suitable fuel cells such as Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC). In other words, when hydrogen energy is used, vehicles that emit only water vapor from the exhaust pipes to the environment circulate on the roads. However, in order to eliminate the storage problem (related to temperature and pressure), which is one of the disadvantages of hydrogen energy, many studies have been encountered recently on the solid storage of hydrogen in chemical hydrides. These are chemical boron hydrides such as NaBH4 [6–8], NH3BH3 [9–13], KBH4 [14], LiBH4 [15–17], (CH3)2NHBH3 [18]. These hydrides not only have the property of storing hydrogen, but also release hydrogen gas from the solvent through reactions such as hydrolysis and methanolysis in the presence of a suitable catalyst at room conditions. The most popular of these boron hydrides is NaBH4 [19–21]. Considering the advantages of sodium borohydride such as high hydrogen content (10.8%), stability in alkaline environment, non-flammability, non-toxicity and recyclability, it becomes clearer how popular sodium boron hydride (NaBH4) [22–25]. As it is known, in the presence of suitable catalysts to produce hydrogen gas (H2) from boron hydrides, a hydrolysis reaction takes place as follows [26].
(1)

As seen in the hydrolysis reaction above, catalysts play an important role in hydrogen production. The priority here is that sodium borohydride is pH > 9 in the solution medium. That is, the solution medium must be stabilized and the alkali solution must be hydrolyzed in the presence of suitable catalysts. Although precious metals such as Pt, Ru and Rh are generally used as catalysts, they are not considered suitable in practical applications due to their high costs [27–29]. For this reason, in hydrogen (H2) production, studies are carried out to develop metal catalysts (Co, Fe, Cu, Cr, Ni) and their alloys by using metal catalysts that are generally not valuable but close to metals with high activities [12,30–33]. More importantly, there have been recent studies on lightweight catalysts to reduce the weight of the hydrogen fuel system. In this study, the catalytic effect of the catalyst was investigated for the first time by adding graphene oxide and caffeine to both increase the performance of the catalyst and to obtain a layered structure consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQD). It is a lightweight catalyst support material containing Co metal under hydrothermal conditions.

Graphene oxide is a layer with honeycomb lattice sp2 bonded carbon atoms having a large surface area of approximately (2600 m2/g), superior electrical conductivity, high thermal stability, superior physical properties, and functional groups containing hydroxyl and epoxide groups at the edges. As it is known, reduced graphene oxide can be synthesized by easily preparing graphene oxide from graphite by Hummers' method. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) play an important role in catalytic efficiency in terms of fast electron transfer, containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, and having the same properties as graphene oxide in terms of high surface area and electronic properties [34–37].

Since it is costly, time-consuming and difficult in obtaining all these experimental parameters and in determining the properties of the CQD/GO@Co catalyst produced with nanoparticles and in forming of the structure of the solution environment by experimental methods, computational methods have been started to be used by researchers in determining the production and characterization processes of catalysts [38]. Recently, Molecular Dynamics (MD) and Monte Carlo simulation methods have been used to determine many physical and chemical properties of a system on an atomic scale. According to MD method, the motions of atoms under the influence of forces formed as a result of appropriately defined potential energy functions at the nano-scale level of systems are examined depending on time. In addition, by creating equations of motion according to Newton’s second law, the properties of the material can be determined under different conditions. In particular, simulations based on density functional theory (DFT) have many advantages in determining the electronic, optical and thermodynamic properties of materials [39].

Here for the hydrolysis of NaBH4, for the first time, the production and characterization of the (CQD/GO@Co) catalyst obtained from caffeine and graphene oxide obtained under hydrothermal conditions was examined and discussed in this study. Here, the effects of different hydrothermal conditions (temperature and residence time), NaBH4 concentration, different alkaline solution and different temperatures on hydrogen (H2) production were investigated. The rest of this work is designed as follows: in the second part, experimental materials, syntheses, material characterization method, hydrolysis step and the method used in molecular modeling theoretically are mentioned. In the third part, material characterization was analyzed using BET, TEM and XRD. Field emission scanning electron micrographs (FESEM) were taken by Field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Sigma). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra were obtained from Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscope (Zeiss, Sigma). In addition, molecular dynamics calculations that can explain the effect of cobalt on H2 production in detail are explained in this section. Finally, the most important highlights are mentioned in the discussion section.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2.6H20), graphite, potassium permanganate (KMnO4), caffeine, hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich used in the synthesis and hydrolysis. Ultrapure water was used in sample preparation. Graphene oxide (GO) synthesis was performed according to the Hummers' method [40]. Then, CQD was synthesized from caffeine, which is kept in an autoclave in distilled water at 180 °C for 6 h, under hydrothermal conditions. The synthesized CQDs were loaded into GO by reflux method at 150 °C for 12 h. Co atoms were impregnated with the GOCQD composition at 1000 rpm at room conditions [41]. CQD/GO@Co catalyst structure synthesis was achieved as a result of reduction process under sodium borohydride after metal process. During the catalyst synthesis process, drying is carried out in an inert gas atmosphere to prevent undesirable reactions due to catalyst activity. In this study, drying was carried out in a nitrogen gas atmosphere at a flow rate of 20 mL/min after the reduction and filtration process. As a result of the drying process, the catalyst structure was elucidated.

For the obtained catalyst sample; surface areas were calculated using the Brunauere Emmette Teller (BET) method over nitrogen isotherm, and the pore size distributions were determined according to the Barrett Joyner Halenda (BJH) method. Morphology of the catalyst precursors were identified by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F20, Philips). X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed on a Rigaku RINT-2000 X-ray diffraction (XRD-Bruker, 2θ = 5–80, λ = 1.54 nm, with Cu Ka radiation at 40 kV/30 mA) to analyze the microstructure and composition of the samples. The morphologies of the materials were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) were taken by Field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Sigma). The elemental composition and their dispersion were captured by energy dispersive. X-ray (EDX) spectra were obtained from Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscope (Zeiss, Sigma). CQD/GO@Co catalyst structure identification process, catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 was carried out in the presence of the synthesized CQD/GO@Co catalyst. The hydrogen generation experiments were conducted using the water displacement method. Initially, 10 ml of the solution containing 2 wt% NaBH4 and 5.0 wt% NaOH, 10 mg catalyst, kept constant at 30 °C, was prepared in a closed bottle equipped with a temperature controller. Next, we added 10 mg of the prepared catalyst to the solution bottle to start the hydrolysis reaction. An outlet tube was connected to the flask to collect the hydrogen gas. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F20, Philips) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed on a Rigaku RINT-2000 X-ray diffraction meter with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å) to analyze the microstructure and composition of the samples. Field emission scanning electron micrographs (FESEM) were taken by Field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Sigma). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra were obtained from Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (Zeiss, Sigma).

After CQD/GO@Co catalyst structure identification process, catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 was carried out in the presence of the synthesized CQD/GO@Co catalyst. In hydrolysis, respectively; NaOH effect for solution alkalinity, catalyst amount, hydrogen source concentration and temperature effect measurements were performed for reaction kinetic parameters. Finally, the repeated use of the catalyst was examined and the effect measurements of the CQD/GO@Co catalyst on hydrogen production were completed. In this study, for the hydrogen obtained from the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride; first, the alkalinity of the NaBH4 solution, the amount of catalyst, the concentration of the solution, the reaction temperature, and the carbon quantum dots obtained from caffeine and the optimum amount of Co metal attached to graphene oxide were investigated in order to increase the catalytic activity of the catalyst. At the same time, to determine whether the catalyst used for hydrogen obtained from the hydrolysis of NaBH4 is heterogeneous or not, a certain amount of mercury (Hg) poisoning experiment was carried out into the solution [12,42].

Since this simulation involves the interactions of a large number of atoms and molecular systems, a number of approaches must be introduced in theoretical calculation to overcome the computational cost. The tight-binding Hamiltonian, first described by Grimme et al. (GFNxTB) [43], shows sufficient precision to achieve charge convergence. The model used in this study is the quantum mechanical nonSccGFN1xTB approach, which is one of these GFNxTB series. Since non-covalent interactions are supported by semi-empirical parameters in this study, they provide faster charge convergence compared to models such as DFT and DFTB. This quick calculation is entirely dependent on the total Hamiltonian energy. The only difference between the quantum mechanical DFTB3 model, which takes into account third-order charge derivatives and the non-Scc-GFN1-xTB model is the difference in the Eel expression in Equation (2). The common feature in all models is that the total Hamiltonian energy is the sum of electronic energy (indexed by “el”), repulsive coulomb interaction (indexed by “rep”), dispersion energy (indexed by “disp”) and halogen-bonding (indexed by “XB”) the terms:
(2)
E =

(3)

The first term here is the total energy of all electrons belonging to occupation valence orbitals. The second and third terms represent the contribution of the second and third energy derivatives, respectively. The last term represents the sum of the energy of the free electrons [43]. The tight-binding approach, considering the interaction between two atoms, has a feature that prevents much more computational cost than the DFT model. It represents all of the electrons in the system under a Coulomb effect as a superposition of their respective atomic orbitals. In the Non-Scc-GFN1xTB model, there is no need to evaluate the second and third terms in Equation (3). Instead, the Slater Atomic Orbital function is used for coupling, and this is expressed in the first-order Hamiltonian by the Extended Hünkel Theory. Thus, self-consistency contributions based on Mulliken charges terms, which cause the increase of computational cost, do not need to be taken into account. It is seen that not all factors that cause this model to increase the computational cost considerably are taken into account. Energy gradients (second and third derivative operators) that are not taken into account are solved by a good optimization tight-binding approach. Although this approach shows a little bad behavior at oscillation frequencies compared to the GFNxTB model, it shows a very good approach, especially in the optimization of transition metals, which is also important in this study. Charge and equilibrium bond distances of the system may be slightly less accurate since they are supported by experimental data and defined by Force-Field Functions. However, as in this study, it makes it possible to research in molecular dynamics calculations of systems covering hundreds of atoms. It also covers up some SCC failures. As for the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th terms in Equation (2), they are all that the GFNxTB models have in common. The second term provides the electronic repulsive interaction to the dynamic simulation with the contribution of the effective charges of the nuclei of the atoms. The third term contributes to the total Hamiltonian energy with the D3 dispersion, which gives better results in the GFN1 model, especially in non-covalent interactions. Since the non-Scc-GFN1xTB model does not give good results in weak halogen bonds, the fourth term showing halogen contributions makes an important contribution in this study [43–46]. These last three terms have been discussed in detail in previous work by some of the authors of the study [47].

This study consists of 3 different chapters covering material generation, H2 gas production environment and their molecular dynamics simulation. The performance of the material, which is considered as a catalyst, to produce H2 gas depends on the cobalt metal ion working in harmony with the carbon quantum dots formed from caffeine. The most important feature that distinguishes it from other studies in the literature is that the cobalt metal ion in the catalyst can be found in many geometric configurations on the caffeine surface with very low binding energies. However, the most stable structure in these distributions is the presence of cobalt metal ions on the caffeine surface without overlapping, that is, without metallic bonding to each other. This was determined by molecular dynamic modeling (see Fig. 1 ). In the simulation, the element cobalt was bound to nitrogen and oxygen atoms in caffeine. Although the detailed representation is not shown in the figure, the cobalt element shows the configuration in which it makes metallic bonding to each other in the regions where the energy is high. During the simulation, due to the electrostatic effect of the H2O molecules on the cobalt element, the lack of stability of the cobalt element on the atoms with which it has weak H-bonds in caffeine, and therefore, the fact that these atoms also display single bonds between each other increases the weak H-bond density on the surface. It provides the movement of the solution medium on the catalyst surface with lower viscosity [48,49].

The above-mentioned in Fig. 1 contains the simulation of a single caffeine molecule and cobalt elements in H2O environment. The caffeine-cobalt relationship has a very important place in the material characterization. Whether the doped feature of the cobalt element is homogeneous or not depends on the relationship of the carbon quantum dots with the cobalt material. The placement of the cobalt material in the CQD/GO layer mentioned in the rest of the study, and the stable geometric distribution of the cobalt element not only on the caffeine surface but also among the caffeine according to the reference model, causes an increase in the surface area in the CQD/GO@Co layer. This situation was experimentally determined by the Bruauner-Emmett-Teller (BET) method of the surface area of the synthesized CQD/GO@Co catalyst. The surface area and pore diameter of prepared catalysts are given in Table 1 . As can be seen from Table 1, it is seen that both the surface area and the pore volume of the cobalt supported catalyst have increased. The probable reason for this is that the pores of the obtained carbon quantum dots are micro-structured and the Co metal closes these gaps, increasing the catalytic activity of the catalyst. This is observed in most studies with cobalt supported catalysts [50]. This increase in surface area and pore diameter means that atoms in the solution medium create large interaction areas on the surface. Although the pure distribution of the cobalt element shows a very large surface area and pore diameter feature, they can only show H-bond properties in surface elements due to the metallic bonding between each other.

Figs. 2 and 3
shows the isotherm curves according to the IUPAC classification obtained in the nitrogen adsorption/desorption technique. As is known, the adsorption isotherm gives information about the adsorption capacity of the material. Looking at Figs. 2 and 3, it is seen that both isotherm types comply with IV and V isotherms according to IUPAC classification. Types IV and V represent single and multilayer adsorption and capillary condensation [51]. Adsorption isotherms, where the adsorption heat of the first layer is higher than the condensation heat and capillary condensation is high, resemble this curve. Fig. 2 conforms to Type IV. The reason for this, as seen in the Figure, is that adsorption and desorption isotherms follow different paths, which is called adsorption hysteresis. Fig. 3 resembles Type V. Adsorption isotherms in mesoporous solids with low adsorption power resemble this type of curves. These results support the TEM images in Table 1 and Fig. 4 . That is, the large pore volume agglomerates consisting only of Co (Fig. 4a and b) were replaced by Co nono clusters with very small pore volumes and homogeneous distribution attached to the CQD/GO surface (Fig. 4e and f).

Another experimental parameter demonstrating material characterization in this study is TEM analysis (See Fig. 4). Here, TEM image of Co metal (a-b), TEM image of metal-free carbon quantum dots composed of reduced graphene oxide and caffeine (c-d), and TEM image of carbon quantum dots composed of reduced graphene oxide and caffeine supported by Co metal (e-f) are shown. In the TEM image of the Co catalyst, it is seen that agglomeration occurs on top of each other. The binding of these metallic ions reduces the cobalt effectiveness in any external interaction. Only in the presence of the element cobalt, cobalt atoms form their own crystal structure. This is seen in (a-b). When one looks at the images supported by graphene oxide in the presence of carbon quantum dots obtained from caffeine, it is seen in sections c and d of the figure that the catalyst is homogeneously dispersed in thin long pieces, that is, it covers entirely catalyst surface. In this optimization of the layer, the fact that the bonding of the caffeine molecules to each other, especially through the nitrogen and oxygen endpoints, provides the most stable electronic energy, contributed to the homogeneity of the distribution. These atoms provide a connection both to each other and to the layer. In addition, it prevents clumping of the GO layer with its flat plate conformer. Sections e and f of Fig. 4 show the TEM shape in which the cobalt element is doped into the CQD/GO layer. Here, it is seen that the cobalt metal ions exhibit a homogeneous distribution on the caffeine molecular structures (on the rod-shaped regions), and this situation is also supported by the simulation result in Fig. 1. This indicates that the material has been successfully designed for the catalyst effect to work well.

Fig. 5 shows the XRD image of metal-free carbon quantum dots composed of reduced graphene oxide and caffeine (a) the unlayered XRD image of Co metal (b), and the XRD results of carbon quantum dots composed of reduced graphene oxide and caffeine supported by Co metal (c). As can be seen from the figure, it is seen that the carbon quantum dots consisting of caffeine give a peak at 2θ = 12o and 25o Bragg diffraction angle [52], their size range is around ± 3.1 nm and corresponds to the (002) phase. It is seen that Co metal gives 2θ = 34o and 52o peaks in the XRD image, and it is almost the same as 2θ = 25o, 34o and 52o peaks in the XRD image of the cobalt supported catalyst. In the “a” part of the Figure, it shows that graphene oxide and caffeine have an effective crystal structure. The building shows a very distinctive feature.

In the “b” part of the Figure, it shows that the cobalt elements doped on graphite have effective crystal properties. The peaks are distinct and sharp. In part “c” of the Figure, caffeine has completely disappeared.

Cobalt metal ions infiltrated caffeine with a very good diffusion effect. This shows that cobalt elements can be found among caffeine. However, the cobalt GO material did not penetrate into it. In the GO sharp peak, any sharpness did not change after the addition of cobalt. It has shifted from 20° to 25° just due to the agglomeration of the layer. Looking at part “c” of the Figure, Cobalt has lost its crystalline structure. It has gained an amorphous feature. The peaks became broad both in pure cobalt compound and in terms of caffeine binding. This XRD distribution caused the cobalt element to diffuse into and on the caffeine very well and the caffeine peak to disappear. This situation better explains the homogeneous dispersion of the cobalt crystals of the material. Although caffeine was not visible in the triple combination (part c), the sharp graphite peak at 25° in the graphite caffeine compound became broader. At this point, it shows that the graphite-caffeine relationship continues. However, the CQD/GO structure has moved away from the crystalline structure. Cobalt has not broken the connection of these two. The graphite peak at 43o shown in the “a” part of the figure disappeared when the cobalt elements entered the caffeine as well. That is, the GO layer cannot be detected at this Bragg diffraction angle. Another important point to be mentioned here is the very high catalyst efficiency, which indicates that, after catalyst synthesis, atomic-level coalescences occur due to combustion in the interaction of the catalyst with air. The corresponding catalyst activity was made useable by controlled oxidation. This situation is clearly seen in the CoO peak in the XRD graph Fig. 5(c).

From the images in Fig. 6 a, it is clearly seen that large gaps are formed with the layered and leaf-like structure of GO and the homogeneous distribution of Co nanoparticles doped on GO. From the image in Fig. 6b; It is seen that by supporting Co nanoparticles with CQD, these gaps are closed and smaller pores are formed, and they are not subjected to any particle growth and maintain their initial morphology. This can be seen as a result of detailed examination of the distribution of white dots on GO layers. In Fig. 6c and d, in the elemental distribution analysis performed with EDX during the FESEM analysis, it was determined that the nanocomposite structure consisted largely of C, O, and Co elements, but there was also a small amount of Cl element. It is thought that Cl remains as an impurity formed during the preparation of GO by the modified Hummers' method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution environment and temperature

ce:section.ce:para:
The solution environment effect is the most important parameter examined to clearly reveal the effect of the catalyst in catalytic reactions. For the change of the solution environment; substances such as NH4Cl, HCl and Acetic acid are used [53,54]. However, since the activity of the Co@CQD/GO catalyst was intended to be tested in this study, NaOH was used for the solution environment concentration. As is known, sodium borohydride can self-hydrolysis at room temperature under acidic conditions. In order to prevent this, NaOH is added to increase the pH of the solution, ensuring that the pH is > 9 and the solution is stable. However, the solution can be hydrolyzed in the presence of suitable catalysts. In Fig. 7 , the effect of different NaOH concentrations (30 °C, 0.2 g NaBH4, 10 mL solution 10 mg catalyst) on NaBH4 hydrolysis was investigated. The increase in the reaction rate due to the increase in the NaOH mass concentration is evident from Fig. 7. When the hydrogen production rate due to the increase in the NaOH mass concentration is examined, it is determined that the hydrogen production rate, which is 5684 mL/min(g.cat) in 0% NaOH, increases to 49,044 mL/min(g.cat) in 5% NaOH (Fig. 8 ). It has been seen that there are many studies on NaOH added into the solution for hydrogen production by NaBH4 hydrolysis [30,55]. Here, it is shown by the following reaction that hydrogen (H2) formed as a result of the hydrolysis of NaBH4, as well as B(OH)4 - anions cause hydrogen gas to be released. Therefore, it is important to determine the optimum NaOH concentration.
(4)
BH 4
+
4 H

c a t a l y s t

B
( OH )
4
+
4
H
2

Fig. 7 shows how varying amounts of NaOH (with values of 0, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5 and 10%) affect the H2 gas production rate. As can be seen from Equation (4) above, the presence of NaOH amount supports the production of H2 gas. However, as can be seen from the molecular simulation, the presence of a large number of Na+ ions in the environment exists as a collector of free OH− ions. An excess of Na+ ions in a solution medium at a certain rate prevents statistically free OH− ions reaching the NaBH4 molecule. Increasing the NaOH molecule under a constant NaBH4 cannot be expected to give statistically significant results. In the simulation, the bonding of the OH− molecule to the boron atom allows the H atoms to be released from the boron atom. This reaction is the most important step in the system that lowers the activation energy and releases the H atom.

As the NaOH concentration increases, the number of free Na+ ions in the environment increases. Compared to the interaction diameter of water molecules, the large van der Waals interaction diameter of the Na atom prevents the hydrolysis of water molecules. That is, each NaOH molecule releases Na+ and H+ into the reaction medium as the resulting of hydrolysis, after a certain rate, base saturation occurs depending on the ratio of the reactants. After a certain increase value, the Na cation block develops in the system. This also reduces the possibility of free H+ ions interacting with each other. This contains free H+ ions in the environment. The increased NaOH molecules are not used efficiently for H2 production. In Table 2 , it can be seen that pH values converge to a certain value. This situation is also compatible with the simulation results. Another possible situation is that it has been reported that the reaction rate decreases as the surface area required for the reaction decreases as a result of the increase in catalyst particle size at high pH values [56]. To support these theories, the pH of solutions with different NaOH concentrations was examined. These values were recorded in Table 2. As can be seen from the table, base saturation converges towards a value. In Fig. 8, it is seen that the H2-production rate in the amount of 5% NaOH is more effective than the others.

Molecular dynamic simulation of Equation (4) is shown in Fig. 9 . Molecular dynamic temperature was used instead of catalyst in the simulation. In the system, a simulation was started with particles with a high kinetic energy, which fell from high temperature 800 °C to room temperature in 1 ps. The stable energy of the molecular dynamic movement is proportional to the rate of covalent bonding with the boron element, caused by the OH negative ions (resulting from the breakdown of H2O and NaOH) formed in the solution environment. The H2 molecule was formed by the statistical combination (interaction) of the H atoms released from the boron element. The molecular dynamics in Fig. 9 were performed using 6(NaBH4) 3(NaOH) 96(H2O) molecules, in three-dimensional gamma only-k space in 1 fs time step, with a Berendsen thermostat at room temperature with electronic temperature (Fermi occupation). The simulation took about 9 ps. The newly formed B(OH)2 molecule in the medium made an H-bond with a free Na positive ion with a bond distance of about 3 Å. The increase in energy increase after about 8 ps indicates the increase in the amount of H released. H atoms moving away from boron can form H2 by combining of boron with H atoms in the water molecule. Statistically, according to the probability of their collision, H atoms combine with each other. The OH in NaOH exhibits its other task in the solution, triggering the formation of H2. It decomposes water as OH + H. It also provides the formation of Hydronium (H3O) + ion. The released OH ions can also form the short-lived H3O2 molecule. The most important condition in all these processes is that boron and released Na ions form H-bonds with water molecules. In the molecular dynamics simulation in Fig. 9, approximately 10H2 gases are released. The Na atom belonging to the Na–B(OH)2 molecule shown with the blue ring in the related Figure is one of the Na + atoms that move freely in the solution environment. It interacts with any free Na + ion in the aqueous medium. It takes about 3 ps for the stability of the system. Even if the kinetic energy increase in the system at the end of the simulation (increase in the free H atom), it does not affect the molecular structure stability of Na–B(OH)2. This stability shows that the Non-sccGFN1xTB model works well.

Catalyst amounts of 5, 15, 20 and 25 mg were used to obtain hydrogen from the hydrolysis of NaBH4 (with temperature of 30 °C, NaBH4 of 0.2g, 5% NaOH kept constant). 10% of these catalyst amounts consist of cobal metal. This is a very good ratio for green synthesis. Because this means that the rate of heavy metals that are likely to be released into the nature is reduced by 90%. Fig. 10 shows the hydrogen production rate per gram per minute of the amount of catalyst in the bar graph within the Figure, with the effect of the amount of catalyst to obtain hydrogen from the hydrolysis of NaBH4. As can be seen, the usage of catalyst around 10–15 mg gives the highest activity value for Co@CQD/GO catalyst. The relationship between catalyst activity and hydrogen source concentration is one of the most important factors determining catalytic activity. Studies conducted to increase catalytic activity have significantly reduced the metal usage in the catalyst over time [34,57]. As stated in the title of NaOH effect, the best H2 gas production was obtained in 10 mg solution according to the initial solution conditions. The solution medium in Fig. 9 is interacted with an optimized semi-crystalline formation consisting of 1 GO layer, 4 caffeine and 6 cobalt elements in Fig. 11 . For a better view of the layer media in Fig. 11, the solution and the layer media are shown separately from each other. During the molecular dynamics time of 20 ps, a total of 14H2 molecules stably completed the simulation. Although many H atoms are free in the environment during the simulation, the mean free paths of these atoms, which do not statistically collide, are short-range. In other words, it becomes stable as soon as possible by interacting with the atom which it is separated or with a neighboring negative ion. The reason why H2 production is faster than the free H atom is that the OH negative ion in NaOH molecules interacts with boron, causing 2 H atoms (one from OH and one from Boron) to combine. For the calculated 1 fs time step, this interaction is treated as the formation of the H2 molecule.

In the most efficient catalyst combination mentioned above, 5% NaOH, 10 mg of CQD/GO@Co catalyst was kept constant at 30 °C in order to research the effect of NaBH4. To see the effect of NaBH4 reactant, the effect of different (1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, 5%, 7.5% and 10%) NaBH4 concentrations on hydrogen production against time is given in Fig. 12 . When looking at the effect of different (1–10%) NaBH4 concentrations on hydrogen production rates, respectively, HGR values are 38,538, 49,045, 42,485, 47,840, 48,708, 47,100, 40,272 in the unit of mL/min.(g.cat). It is seen that the hydrogen volumes obtained at all 4 concentrations are the same. On the other hand, as the NaBH4 concentration increases, it is seen that the hydrolysis times increase and the hydrogen production rate decreases. It is known that this is due to the fact that sodium metaborate formed during the hydrolysis of NaBH4 makes hydrogen transfer difficult due to its low solubility at low temperatures [58]. As with the increase in the amount of NaOH, it reaches its saturation capacity. Since NaBH4 also works in parallel with NaOH, it should not be forgotten that this performance of NaBH4 depends on 5% NaOH. Considering the initial concentrations, 2% NaBH4 provides the best catalytic effect.

As can be seen in Fig. 11, 4 moles of H2 gas are released at all NaBH4 concentrations. It is observed that as the NaBH4 concentration increases, the gas volume obtained increases, but accordingly, as the NaBH4 concentration increases, after 2% NaBH4 concentration, the HGR are close to each other and then decrease. It is thought that the reason for this decrease is that the solution viscosity increases at high NaBH4 concentrations, the amount of product NaBO2, which increases with the progress of the reaction, also contributes to this increase and also limits access to active sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of hydrolysis temperature and kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Catalytic hydrolysis of sodium borohydride was carried out in the presence of various temperatures (by keeping 5% NaOH, 5.2 mmol NaBH4, 10 mg CQD/GO@Co, 30 °C and 10 ml solution constant). Fig. 13 shows the amount of hydrogen released versus the time graph for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 at various temperatures in the range of 30–60 °C. Here, it is clearly seen that the production rate of hydrogen gas increases with increasing temperature. The probable reason for this situation is that the effective number of collisions results in more products due to the increasing kinetic energy of the particles with increasing temperature. It is observed that the hydrogen production rate increased from 49,044 mL/min.(g.cat) to 140,145 mL/min.(g.cat) when the reaction temperature is increased from 30 °C to 60 °C. The graph in the Figure shows the activation energy graph of the CQD/GO@Co catalyst. The activation energy equation for the hydrolysis reaction here is given in the equation below.
(5)

here k, A, Ea, R and T represent reaction rate constant, Arrhenius constant, activation energy, gas constant and absolute temperature, respectively. Reaction temperature (1/T) versus lnK linear plot was made and activation energy was found to be 20.65 kJ mol−1. The calculated Ea value is compared with some studies in the literature and given in Table 3 below. The nth order kinetics was used to determine the reaction order.

In the integration of Equation (6), the activation in Equation (5) has been shown with the change of concentrations depending on temperature. Thus, the reaction rate concept in Equation (5) is shown as follows.

The rate constant (k) can be calculated from the slope of the line obtained from the graph plotted against time. The grade (n) of the CQD/GO@Co catalyst used to obtain hydrogen from NaBH4 hydrolysis was determined to be 0.15. The activation enthalpy (ΔH) and activation entropy (ΔS) (activated by catalysis) of the reaction are determined when the given Eyring-Polanyi equation, which is obtained from the nth order reaction equation
(8) ln k/T = − ΔH/R.1/T + ln kB/h + ΔS/R

Here kB is Boltzmann constant (1.31 × 10−23 J/K), h is Planck constant (6.626 × 10−34 Js), and R is ideal gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1). As seen in Fig. 15
, when ln (k/T) versus 1/T graph data is written in Equation (8), it is determined as ΔH: 2.64 kJ mol−1, ΔS: 108.97 J mol−1. The low activation enthalpy of the reaction and the negative value of the reaction activation entropy indicate that catalysis occurs at very low energies.

The most important difference between this study and the Ref. 49 in the literature is the contribution of the GO layer. Since the cobalt element bonds to the N and oxygen atoms in caffeine, weak interaction occurs in the bonds between the caffeine and the GO layer. This creates a catalyst effect in the GO layer. Fig. 15 shows molecular dynamics simulation with the lowest atomic ratio of the ratios used to understand the relevant system depending on the temperature. In this system, 500H2O, 10 NaBH4, 6 NaOH, 6 cobalt elements, 4 caffeine and 1GO are the lowest ratio in the substrate solution system.

Since the number of free H atoms increases due to the increasing kinetic energy and collision energy as the temperature increases, there is an increase in the number of H2 molecules when H atoms meet, according to the statistical probability of collision. Experimentally, as seen in Fig. 13, the rate of H2 molecule production at temperatures of 30–40 and 50–60° is related to the rate of hydrolysis of the water molecule. The reason why every temperature up to 0.1 ps releases the same amount of H2 is related to the density of the water molecule in the system. This situation varies in proportion to the fact that the interaction (mean free path) between them is the same and the same time step and number of molecules and coordinators are used for each temperature system. This difference between temperatures theoretically starts after 0.2 ps. Changes in internal energies between water molecules at certain temperature ranges cause changes in electrical dipoles. This increase in vibration frequencies contributes to the total energy in the system. Comparing Fig. 14 with Figs. 9 and 11, which are other molecular dynamics representations, it is clearly seen that the amount of H2 produced increases with the increasing number of molecules in the solution medium (if one compares the temperature of 30°, which is common to these three Figures). It is clearly seen that around 10H2 production takes place in Fig. 9 without catalysis, 14 with catalysis in Fig. 11, and around 24 with high solvent ratio in Fig. 15. In this last way the reaction time is considerably less than the others. It is clearly seen that this value can be much higher when the same simulation time is expected. However, in such high atomic systems it would take several months to wait for 20 ps. Therefore, the aim in this multi-molecular system is to see the reason for the difference between 30 and 40 °C and 50–60 °C depending on the temperature.

The reusability and stability of the CQD/GO@Co nano-catalyst as a heterogeneous catalyst were investigated in NaBH4 hydrolysis. For this reason, hydrolysis experiments were carried out at 30 °C using 2% NaBH4, 10 ml solution, 10 mg catalyst to test the durability. CQD/GO@Co nano-catalyst shows that it can be successfully reused at least 6 times without significant loss in catalytic activity, as can be seen from the graph obtained in Fig. 16 . The catalyst obtained retains only 32.5% of its initial catalytic activity. The reason for this is that the filtrate obtained by centrifuging the solid material (CQD/GO@Co) after 6 cycles causes a decrease in the accessibility of the Co nanoparticles to the active sites due to the passivation of the metal surface by the metaborate ions that accumulate in the solution as the reaction progresses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mercury poisoning as heterogeneity test of CQD/GO@Co catalyst in the hydrolysis of NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) poisoning was carried out to determine whether the catalyst used in the production of hydrogen from the catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 is heterogeneous or homogeneous. As seen in Fig. 17 , when mercury was injected into the solution, it was determined that the hydrogen output remained constant after a certain period of time, and the synthesized catalyst was a heterogeneous catalyst. The basis of this concept of poisoning is related to the fact that cobalt metal ions have a very good tendency to form compounds with the element Hg. If the cobalt metal ion could effectively use the surface of the caffeine structure (that is, if it could spread homogeneously), the probability of forming a compound of the Hg element would be reduced. Here, the caffeine molecule acts as a protective structure for the cobalt element. However, when the cobalt elements used are in solid phase density (that is, the catalysis is in a heterogeneous form), the caffeine surface is not used fully effectively. In such cases, cobalt metal ions, which do not interact with the caffeine structure, are inhibited by Hg and H2 production activities are reduced. In such a case, caffeine cannot act as a protector. Fig. 17 shows the effective decrease in the H2 production rate 5 min after Hg addition.

The study highlights the importance of using the orientation of caffeine with the cobalt metal ion as a catalysis. Because the study was examined both theoretically and experimentally, cause-effect relationships were more strongly established. Cobalt element’s weak bond with caffeine and its homogeneous spread on the surface of the caffeine molecule as the most stable structure is a critical condition for catalysis. This behavior of cobalt is supported by XRD and TEM results. In this way, weak H bonding of water molecules with oxygen atoms both on and around the layer accelerates the hydrolysis process of water molecules. At the same time, the covalent bonding of free OH negative ions with the boron atom in the NaBH4 molecule by using the NaOH molecule in solution contributed to the increase of free H atoms in the solution environment. Since the NaOH molecule is rapidly ionized in the water solution, the free OH negative ions try to make covalent bonds with the boron atoms in the NaBH4 molecule. This process is much faster than the covalent bond of the H2O molecule with the boron atom. Here, a good adjustment of the amount of Na-containing NaBH4 and NaOH emerges in molecular dynamics calculations, where it is important for efficient use of the material. The free Na positive ion tries to keep the free OH negative ions around. This is undesirable in the case of hydrolysis. Excess Na forms positive blocks in solution. Temperature, on the other hand, has a positive effect on hydrolysis as high collision energies and electronic temperatures increase. It also accelerates the interactions with high electrical dipole moments that occur with high vibration frequencies in the H2O molecule. This behavior causes a significant difference between 40 and 50°. This is reflected in the production of H2. As a catalyst, cobalt metal ions contribute to the production of H2 by 40% even in such a small structure, while the contribution of over 100% (even at about 0.4 ps) in the increasing amount of solution indicates the repeated use of the catalyst. The effect on catalysis performance is considered worthy of experimentation, as other metal ions can be shown to bind weakly with caffeine [67].

In possible situations that may arise when the solution environment containing the NaBH4 molecule interacts with the catalyst, some decomposition mechanisms of the NaBH4 molecule are as follows. First of all, NaBH4 is present in any solution environment as in Reaction (9).

As seen in Reaction 10, any decrease in the number of protons negatively affects the progression of this NaBH4 -HR. However, the slowing down of NaBH4-HR can be prevented by increasing the number of protons involved in this reaction. In this study, the role of Carbon quantum dot technology on H2 production was observed. However, carbon quantum dots are used for various purposes [68–70]. Therefore, the use of carbon quantum dots, which are very effective for H2 production in this study, for other purposes raises curiosity.
